title: Tavarez v. State
summary: Tavarez appealed his conviction, challenging the trial court’s admission of witness testimony. The Supreme Court of Georgia clarified the application of the improper bolstering rule, cabining it strictly to contexts where testimony is admitted as a prior consistent statement. The Court affirmed, holding that the appellant failed to meet the burden of showing reversible error in the appellate record.

title: Golden v. Floyd Healthcare Management, Inc.
summary: The estate of a deceased patient sued a healthcare provider alleging negligence. The core issue was whether the medical malpractice statute of repose barred the action. The Court addressed the intersection of substantive liability and procedural filing requirements, clarifying when the repose period commences in wrongful death actions involving alleged medical negligence.

title: Grant v. State
summary: Grant challenged the denial of his motion to suppress evidence obtained during a traffic stop. The Court reviewed the totality of the circumstances to determine if the officer had reasonable suspicion to extend the detention. Affirming the trial court, the Court held that objective facts supported the continued detention, validating the resulting seizure of evidence.

title: Heyward v. State
summary: Heyward was convicted of felony murder after a fatal shooting. The defense argued justification, claiming the trial court erred in its jury instructions regarding lesser included offenses. The Supreme Court of Georgia affirmed, finding that the evidence did not warrant the requested instructions and that the charge as a whole correctly guided the jury on the law of justification.

title: Shahid v. Esaam
summary: This dispute involved an alleged oral agreement for the transfer of business interests. The trial court granted summary judgment for the defendant based on the statute of frauds. The Court of Appeals reviewed the evidentiary record to determine if part performance created an exception to the statute of frauds, ultimately reversing and finding genuine issues of material fact regarding the existence of the agreement.

title: Profet v. State
summary: Profet filed a motion for an out-of-time appeal, alleging trial counsel provided ineffective assistance by failing to properly advise him on the sentencing consequences of his plea. The Court examined the prejudice prong under Strickland. Remanding the case, the Court held that the record below was insufficient to determine if the defendant would have proceeded to trial but for counsel’s advice.

title: Woods v. State
summary: Woods appealed his conviction for felony murder and aggravated assault. He contended that the admission of specific forensic evidence violated his confrontation rights. The Court affirmed, concluding that any potential constitutional error in the admission of the evidence was harmless beyond a reasonable doubt, as it was cumulative of other overwhelming evidence of guilt.
